content:
# Luke Chapter 16 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 108-109 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_016_page_001.jpg, LUK_016_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And he was saying also to the disciples, "A certain man was rich, who had a steward, and this [one] was accused to him as squandering his possessions.

**2.** And having called him, he said to him, 'What [is] this I hear concerning you? Give back the account of your stewardship, for you are no longer able to be-steward.'

**3.** And the steward said within himself, 'What shall I do, because my lord is taking-away the stewardship from me? I am not strong-[enough] to dig; I am ashamed to beg.

**4.** I have known what I shall do, so that when I am removed from the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.'

**5.** And having called-to [him] each one of the debtors of his own lord, he was saying to the first, 'How much do you owe to my lord?'

**6.** And he said, 'A hundred baths of oil.' And he said to him, 'Take your written-document, and having sat down, quickly write fifty.'

**7.** Then to another he said, 'And you, how much do you owe?' And he said, 'A hundred cors of wheat.' He says to him, 'Take your written-document and write eighty.'

**8.** And the lord praised the steward of unrighteousness, because he acted shrewdly; because the sons of this age are more shrewd than the sons of the light [are] toward their own generation.

**9.** And I say to you, make for yourselves friends out of the mammon of unrighteousness, so that when it fails, they may receive you into the eternal tents.

**10.** The [one who is] faithful in [the] least [thing] is faithful also in much, and the [one who is] unrighteous in [the] least [thing] is unrighteous also in much.

**11.** If therefore you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will entrust to you the true [riches]?

**12.** And if you have not been faithful in that [which belongs to] another, who will give to you that [which is] your own?

**13.** No household-servant is able to serve two lords; for either he will hate the one and will love the other, or he will hold-to one and will despise the other. You are not able to serve God and mammon."

**14.** And the Pharisees, being lovers-of-money, were hearing all these [things], and they were sneering-at him.

**15.** And he said to them, "You are the [ones] justifying yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts; because the [thing which is] exalted among men [is] an abomination before God.

**16.** The law and the prophets [were] until John; from then the kingdom of God is being-proclaimed-as-good-news, and everyone is forcing-[his-way] into it.

**17.** But it is easier [for] the heaven and the earth to pass-away than [for] one stroke of the law to fall.

**18.** Everyone divorcing his wife and marrying another commits-adultery, and the [one] marrying her who has been divorced from [her] husband commits-adultery.

**19.** And a certain man was rich, and he was clothing-himself [in] purple and fine-linen, feasting sumptuously every day.

**20.** And a certain poor [man] named Lazarus had been laid at his gate, covered-with-sores,

**21.** and desiring to be fed from the [things] falling from the table of the rich [man]; but even the dogs, coming, were licking his sores.

**22.** And it came-to-be [that] the poor [man] died and he was carried-away by the angels into the bosom of Abraham; and the rich [man] also died and was buried.

**23.** And in Hades, having lifted-up his eyes, being in torments, he sees Abraham from afar and Lazarus in his bosoms.

**24.** And he, having called-out, said, 'Father Abraham, have-mercy-on me, and send Lazarus so that he may dip the tip of his finger [in] water and cool my tongue, because I am in-anguish in this flame.'

**25.** And Abraham said, 'Child, remember that you received-in-full your good [things] in your life, and Lazarus likewise the evil [things]; but now here he is comforted, and you are in-anguish.

**26.** And in [addition to] all these [things], between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that the [ones] wishing to cross-over from here to you are not able, nor [may those] from there to us cross-over.'

**27.** And he said, 'I ask you then, father, that you send him to the house of my father,

**28.** for I have five brothers, so that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment.'

**29.** And Abraham says, 'They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them.'

**30.** And he said, 'No, father Abraham, but if someone from [the] dead should go to them, they will repent.'

**31.** And he said to him, 'If they do not hear Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded if someone should rise from [the] dead.'"

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **16:1 — "A certain man was rich" (ανθρωπος τις ην πλουσιος):** Sinaiticus reads ανθρωπος τις ην πλουσιος. The parable is introduced with ελεγεν δε και ("and he was saying also"), the imperfect ελεγεν indicating ongoing teaching — this follows directly from the three parables of chapter 15 addressed to Pharisees and scribes, but is now redirected "to the disciples" (προς τους μαθητας). The word οικονομος ("steward/manager") denotes one entrusted with managing another's household or estate — a position of great responsibility and corresponding accountability. The verb διεβληθη ("was accused") is aorist passive of διαβαλλω ("to throw across/slander/accuse"), the root of "devil" (διαβολος, "the accuser/slanderer"). The steward is accused ως διασκορπιζων ("as squandering"), using the same verb (διασκορπιζω) applied to the prodigal son in 15:13 — verbal linkage binding the two parables together.

2. **16:3 — "I am not strong-[enough] to dig; I am ashamed to beg" (σκαπτειν ουκ ισχυω, επαιτειν αισχυνομαι):** Sinaiticus reads σκαπτειν ουκ ισχυω, επαιτειν αισχυνομαι. The infinitives precede their verbs for emphasis — the activities themselves are foregrounded. The verb σκαπτω ("to dig") refers to manual agricultural labor, particularly digging irrigation ditches or working vineyards. The verb αισχυνομαι ("I am ashamed") is middle voice — the shame is felt internally, self-directed. The steward's self-assessment is simultaneously honest and morally revealing: he is too physically weak for labor and too proud for begging, yet not too proud for fraud. His options are framed not by ethics but by pragmatism.

3. **16:4 — "I have known" (εγνων):** Sinaiticus reads εγνων, aorist of γινωσκω ("to know") — the so-called "ingressive aorist," capturing the moment of insight: "I have come-to-know" or "it has dawned on me." This eureka moment parallels the prodigal's εις εαυτον ελθων ("having come to himself") in 15:17, but with inverted moral direction — the prodigal's insight leads to repentance, the steward's to deeper cunning. The purpose clause ινα...δεξωνται με εις τους οικους αυτων ("so that they may receive me into their houses") reveals his strategy: create a network of obligated debtors who will owe him social reciprocity.

4. **16:6 — "A hundred baths of oil" (εκατον βατους ελαιου):** Sinaiticus reads εκατον βατους ελαιου. The βατος (Hebrew bat) was a liquid measure of approximately 36 liters (roughly 8-9 gallons). A hundred baths of olive oil — approximately 3,600 liters — represents an enormous quantity, the produce of perhaps 150 olive trees, worth approximately 1,000 denarii (nearly three years' wages for a laborer). The reduction to fifty baths cancels a debt of roughly 500 denarii. The instruction "having sat down, quickly write" (καθισας ταχεως γραψον) emphasizes speed — the fraud must be accomplished before the master can intervene. The γραμμα ("written-document") was the debtor's own IOU, written in the debtor's hand, which the steward now has the debtor alter.

5. **16:7 — "A hundred cors of wheat" (εκατον κορους σιτου):** Sinaiticus reads εκατον κορους σιτου. The κορος (Hebrew kor) was a dry measure of approximately 390 liters (roughly 10-11 bushels). A hundred cors of wheat — approximately 39,000 liters — represents the yield of about 100 acres of land, worth roughly 2,500 denarii. The reduction to eighty cors cancels approximately 500 denarii — roughly the same monetary amount as the oil reduction, creating equity between the two debtors. The careful calibration suggests the steward is calculating precisely, not acting randomly.

6. **16:8 — "the lord praised the steward of unrighteousness" (επηνεσεν ο κυριος τον οικονομον της αδικιας):** Sinaiticus reads επηνεσεν ο κυριος τον οικονομον της αδικιας. The identity of ο κυριος ("the lord") is debated: it may refer to the rich man within the parable (who grudgingly admires the steward's cleverness) or to the Lord Jesus commenting on the story. The Hebraic genitive construction "steward of unrighteousness" (οικονομον της αδικιας) means "the unrighteous steward" — adikia qualifies the person, not the office. The verb εφρονησεν ("he acted shrewdly") derives from φρονεω ("to think/be minded"), related to φρονησις ("practical wisdom/prudence"). What is praised is not the steward's dishonesty but his foresight — his ability to use present resources to secure future wellbeing. The comparison "the sons of this age are more shrewd than the sons of the light" (οι υιοι του αιωνος τουτου φρονιμωτεροι υπερ τους υιους του φωτος) uses the Semitic idiom "sons of" to indicate character: those belonging to this present age exercise more practical wisdom in their sphere than those belonging to the light exercise in theirs.

7. **16:9 — "the mammon of unrighteousness" (του μαμωνα της αδικιας):** Sinaiticus reads του μαμωνα της αδικιας. The word μαμωνας is a Greek transliteration of the Aramaic mamona (ממונא), meaning "wealth/property/possessions." The term does not appear in the Hebrew Bible but is common in rabbinic literature (e.g., Mishnah Avot 2:12; Targum Onkelos on Deuteronomy 6:5). The genitive της αδικιας ("of unrighteousness") characterizes mammon as inherently entangled with the fallen world-system — not that all money is stolen, but that wealth belongs to the domain of unrighteousness and must be deliberately redirected toward righteous ends. The phrase "eternal tents" (τας αιωνιους σκηνας) is striking — σκηναι ("tents/tabernacles") echoes the Feast of Tabernacles (Sukkot) and the wilderness tabernacle, temporary dwellings made permanent by the adjective αιωνιος ("eternal/age-lasting").

8. **16:13 — "No household-servant is able to serve two lords" (ουδεις οικετης δυναται δυσι κυριοις δουλευειν):** Sinaiticus reads ουδεις οικετης, adding οικετης ("household-servant") where Matthew 6:24 has simply ουδεις ("no one"). The word οικετης (from οικος, "house") specifies a domestic servant, one who belongs to the household — intensifying the impossibility of dual allegiance. The verb δουλευειν ("to serve-as-slave") implies total ownership, not mere employment. The four verbs — μισησει ("will hate"), αγαπησει ("will love"), ανθεξεται ("will hold-to"), καταφρονησει ("will despise") — are arranged chiastically: hate/love parallels hold-to/despise, showing that divided loyalty is structurally impossible. The final declaration "you are not able to serve God and mammon" (ου δυνασθε θεω δουλευειν και μαμωνα) personifies mammon as a rival deity demanding total allegiance.

9. **16:14 — "lovers-of-money" (φιλαργυροι):** Sinaiticus reads φιλαργυροι υπαρχοντες — "being lovers-of-money." The compound adjective φιλαργυρος (φιλος + αργυρος, "friend of silver") describes an ingrained character trait, not a momentary attitude. The participle υπαρχοντες ("being/existing as") indicates this is their fundamental nature, not a passing condition. The verb εξεμυκτηριζον ("were sneering-at") derives from μυκτηρ ("nose") — literally "to turn up the nose at," expressing contemptuous dismissal. The imperfect tense indicates repeated, sustained mockery. Luke alone among the Gospel writers identifies the Pharisees as φιλαργυροι — a charge supported by Jesus' critiques elsewhere (Matthew 23:25; Mark 12:40; Luke 20:47) and by Josephus's accounts of priestly wealth.

10. **16:15 — "the [thing which is] exalted among men [is] an abomination before God" (το εν ανθρωποις υψηλον βδελυγμα ενωπιον του θεου):** Sinaiticus reads το εν ανθρωποις υψηλον βδελυγμα ενωπιον του θεου. The word βδελυγμα ("abomination") is the LXX's standard translation for Hebrew תועבה (toevah), used for idolatry (Deuteronomy 7:25-26), forbidden sexual acts (Leviticus 18:22), dishonest business practices (Deuteronomy 25:16), and the "abomination of desolation" (Daniel 9:27; 11:31; 12:11). Its application here to what humans "exalt" (υψηλον, "lofty/elevated") is devastating: the very things human society prizes most — wealth, status, prestige — register in God's sight as idolatrous abomination. The reversal is total: human valuation and divine valuation are not merely different but antithetical.

11. **16:16 — "the kingdom of God is being-proclaimed-as-good-news, and everyone is forcing-[his-way] into it" (η βασιλεια του θεου ευαγγελιζεται και πας εις αυτην βιαζεται):** Sinaiticus reads η βασιλεια του θεου ευαγγελιζεται και πας εις αυτην βιαζεται. The verb ευαγγελιζεται ("is being-proclaimed-as-good-news") is present passive — the kingdom is the content of the proclamation, announced as εὐαγγέλιον ("good news"). The verb βιαζεται is either middle ("everyone is forcing his way in") or passive ("everyone is being forced/urged in"). The middle reading suggests violent eagerness — people are pressing into the kingdom with aggressive determination, like a crowd surging through a gate. This verse marks a pivotal salvation-historical transition: "the law and the prophets [were] until John" — not abolished but brought to their fulfillment point. John is the hinge between the old dispensation and the new.

12. **16:17 — "one stroke of the law to fall" (του νομου μιαν κεραιαν πεσειν):** Sinaiticus reads του νομου μιαν κεραιαν πεσειν. The word κεραια ("stroke/hook/serif") literally means "little horn" (diminutive of κερας, "horn") and refers to the tiny decorative strokes or serifs that distinguish similar Hebrew letters (e.g., ב/כ, ד/ר, ה/ח). Jesus affirms that the law is not nullified by the arrival of the kingdom — not the smallest ornamental mark will fall. This verse stands in deliberate tension with verse 16: the law's era as preparatory dispensation has ended, but the law's authority as divine revelation remains absolute. The new era fulfills rather than abolishes.

13. **16:18 — "Everyone divorcing his wife and marrying another commits-adultery" (πας ο απολυων την γυναικα αυτου και γαμων ετεραν μοιχευει):** Sinaiticus reads πας ο απολυων την γυναικα αυτου και γαμων ετεραν μοιχευει. The verb απολυω ("to release/dismiss/divorce") is the standard legal term for divorce in the Gospels. The present participles (ο απολυων...και γαμων) describe anyone who habitually practices this — this is a statement of general principle, not a specific case. The verse functions as a concrete example of verse 17: the Pharisees had liberalized divorce law beyond Moses's intent (cf. the Hillel-Shammai debate in Mishnah Gittin 9:10), and Jesus reasserts the original divine standard. The second clause — "the [one] marrying her who has been divorced from [her] husband" (ο απολελυμενην απο ανδρος γαμων) — extends culpability to the subsequent marriage, treating the original marriage bond as still valid.

14. **16:19 — "purple and fine-linen" (πορφυραν και βυσσον):** Sinaiticus reads ενεδιδυσκετο πορφυραν και βυσσον. The imperfect ενεδιδυσκετο ("was clothing-himself") indicates habitual practice — this was his daily attire, not occasional finery. The πορφυρα ("purple") was the most expensive dye in the ancient world, extracted from the murex sea-snail at enormous cost (Pliny, Natural History 9.124-141); it was associated with royalty (Judges 8:26; Esther 8:15; Mark 15:17) and extreme wealth. The βυσσος ("fine-linen/byssus") was imported Egyptian linen of extraordinary fineness, used for priestly garments (Exodus 28:39 LXX) and royal clothing (1 Chronicles 15:27). The verb ευφραινομενος ("feasting/making-merry") is the same word used for the celebration over the prodigal's return (15:23-24, 32) — but here the merriment is solitary and self-indulgent, καθ ημεραν ("every day"), turning feasting into a lifestyle.

15. **16:20 — "Lazarus" (Λαζαρος):** Sinaiticus reads Λαζαρος, the Greek form of Hebrew Eleazar (אלעזר), meaning "God has helped." This is the only character in any of Jesus' parables who is given a proper name — a detail of great significance. The poor man has a name; the rich man does not. The verb εβεβλητο ("had been laid/thrown") is pluperfect passive of βαλλω ("to throw") — Lazarus did not come to the gate under his own power but had been deposited there, presumably by others. He is helpless, dependent, unable even to position himself. The participle ειλκωμενος ("covered-with-sores/ulcerated") derives from ελκος ("wound/ulcer"), indicating open, weeping sores — a condition of visible physical suffering placed directly at the rich man's gate (πυλων, a large ornamental gateway, not a simple door, indicating a mansion).

16. **16:21 — "the dogs, coming, were licking his sores" (αλλα και οι κυνες ερχομενοι επελειχον τα ελκη αυτου):** Sinaiticus reads αλλα και οι κυνες ερχομενοι επελειχον τα ελκη αυτου. The conjunction αλλα και ("but even/moreover") intensifies the pathos — not only was Lazarus unfed, but dogs (unclean animals in Jewish estimation, cf. 1 Kings 21:19; Psalm 22:16; Matthew 7:6) added to his degradation by licking his open sores. The imperfect επελειχον ("were licking") indicates this was a repeated occurrence. The verb επιθυμων ("desiring") echoes the prodigal's desire to eat pig-food (15:16, επεθυμει) — both figures reach the extremity of unfulfilled longing, but Lazarus never receives even the table-scraps (τα πιπτοντα, "the things falling"). Some manuscripts add χορτασθηναι ("to be satisfied"), but the desire itself remains pointedly unsatisfied.

17. **16:22 — "into the bosom of Abraham" (εις τον κολπον Αβρααμ):** Sinaiticus reads εις τον κολπον Αβρααμ. The κολπος ("bosom/breast/lap") denotes the place of intimate closeness at a banquet — reclining against the chest of the host, as the beloved disciple reclined against Jesus (John 13:23). "Abraham's bosom" became a standard Jewish designation for the blessed afterlife (cf. 4 Maccabees 13:17, "the bosom of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob"). The contrast is total: Lazarus, who lay at the rich man's gate excluded from his banquet, now reclines in the place of highest honor at Abraham's feast. The rich man, who feasted daily, is buried — his funeral is mentioned (εταφη, "was buried"), suggesting elaborate burial rites, but no angels carry him. The reversal of verses 19-22 is the narrative embodiment of verse 15: what was exalted among men (the rich man) is abomination before God; what was despised (Lazarus) is exalted.

18. **16:23 — "in Hades" (εν τω αδη):** Sinaiticus reads εν τω αδη. The word αδης (Hades) is the Greek equivalent of Hebrew שאול (Sheol), the realm of the dead. In this parable, Hades is a place of conscious torment (βασανοις, "torments," from βασανος, originally "touchstone" for testing metals, then "torture/torment"). The detail "having lifted-up his eyes" (επαρας τους οφθαλμους αυτου) indicates he can see across the chasm — visual awareness of the blessed state he cannot reach intensifies his suffering. The phrase "in his bosoms" (εν τοις κολποις αυτου) uses the plural, possibly indicating Abraham's expansive embrace or the fullness of comfort Lazarus now enjoys.

19. **16:24 — "Father Abraham" (πατερ Αβρααμ):** Sinaiticus reads πατερ Αβρααμ. The rich man appeals to his biological descent from Abraham — the very appeal John the Baptist warned against (3:8, "do not begin to say within yourselves, 'We have Abraham [as our] father'"). The verb ελεησον με ("have-mercy-on me") is aorist imperative of ελεεω, the standard cry for mercy in the Psalms and Gospels. The request — to dip "the tip of his finger" (το ακρον του δακτυλου αυτου) in water — is minimal, a single drop of moisture for a tongue in agony. Yet even this is denied. The reversal mirrors Lazarus's earthly experience: as the rich man gave nothing to Lazarus at his gate, so now nothing can be given to the rich man. The verb οδυνωμαι ("I am in-anguish") is present middle/passive — ongoing, sustained suffering.

20. **16:25 — "Child, remember" (τεκνον, μνησθητι):** Sinaiticus reads τεκνον, μνησθητι. Abraham addresses the rich man as τεκνον ("child") — the same tender word the father used for the elder son in 15:31 — acknowledging the familial relationship but not granting the request. The verb μνησθητι ("remember") is aorist passive imperative of μιμνησκομαι — a command to recall what he already knows. The verb απελαβες ("you received-in-full") is aorist of απολαμβανω, a commercial term meaning "to receive in full what is owed" (cf. Philippians 4:18, απεχω, "I have received in full"). The implication is devastating: the rich man's earthly prosperity was his full allotment — he has already been paid. The parallel ομοιως ("likewise") applies to Lazarus: his earthly suffering was his portion of evil things, now reversed.

21. **16:26 — "a great chasm has been fixed" (χασμα μεγα εστηρικται):** Sinaiticus reads χασμα μεγα εστηρικται. The word χασμα ("chasm/gulf") appears only here in the New Testament, derived from χαινω/χασκω ("to yawn/gape open"). The perfect passive εστηρικται ("has been fixed/established") indicates a permanent, irrevocable state — the chasm was established and remains fixed. The verb στηριζω ("to fix/establish/strengthen") implies deliberate divine action — this separation is not accidental but ordained. The consequence is stated with absolute finality: those wishing to cross from either side "are not able" (μη δυνωνται) — the impossibility is ontological, not merely practical.

22. **16:27-28 — "send him to the house of my father" (πεμψης αυτον εις τον οικον του πατρος μου):** Sinaiticus reads πεμψης αυτον εις τον οικον του πατρος μου. The rich man, having failed in his request for personal relief, now asks that Lazarus be sent as a messenger to his five brothers. The verb διαμαρτυρηται ("may warn/solemnly-testify") is a strong compound (δια + μαρτυρεω) — not merely to inform but to testify with urgency and authority. Even in Hades, the rich man treats Lazarus as a servant to be dispatched on errands — the social hierarchy of earth persists in his thinking even when all earthly status has been stripped away. The phrase "this place of torment" (τον τοπον τουτον της βασανου) is his first explicit acknowledgment of where he is.

23. **16:29 — "They have Moses and the prophets" (εχουσιν Μωυσεα και τους προφητας):** Sinaiticus reads εχουσιν Μωυσεα και τους προφητας. Abraham's response identifies the written Scriptures — "Moses" (the Torah/Pentateuch) and "the prophets" (the Nevi'im) — as sufficient witness. The imperative ακουσατωσαν αυτων ("let them hear them") uses ακουω in its full Hebraic sense: not merely to perceive audibly but to hear with obedient response (the Hebrew שמע encompasses both hearing and obeying). The Scriptures are presented not as inadequate testimony requiring supernatural supplementation but as the complete and sufficient witness of God — the problem is not insufficient revelation but insufficient obedience.

24. **16:30 — "if someone from [the] dead should go to them, they will repent" (εαν τις απο νεκρων πορευθη προς αυτους μετανοησουσιν):** Sinaiticus reads εαν τις απο νεκρων πορευθη προς αυτους μετανοησουσιν. The rich man contradicts Abraham with ουχι ("no") — a bold negation addressed to the patriarch himself. His proposal assumes that a miraculous visitation from the dead would accomplish what Scripture could not. The verb μετανοησουσιν ("they will repent") is future active — he is confident of the outcome. This assumption — that spectacular supernatural events produce repentance more effectively than the patient reading of Scripture — is precisely what Jesus repeatedly rejects (cf. Matthew 12:39; 16:4; John 4:48).

25. **16:31 — "neither will they be persuaded if someone should rise from [the] dead" (ουδε εαν τις εκ νεκρων αναστη πεισθησονται):** Sinaiticus reads ουδε εαν τις εκ νεκρων αναστη πεισθησονται. Abraham's final word is definitive: ει Μωυσεως και των προφητων ουκ ακουουσιν ("if they do not hear Moses and the prophets") — the present tense ακουουσιν indicates an ongoing condition of not-hearing. The verb πεισθησονται ("they will be persuaded") is future passive of πειθω — they will not be brought to conviction even by resurrection. The verb αναστη ("should rise") is aorist subjunctive of ανιστημι, the standard resurrection vocabulary (cf. αναστασις). The dramatic irony is immense: Jesus tells this parable knowing that he himself will rise from the dead, and that many will still not believe. Further, a man named Lazarus will be raised from the dead (John 11:1-44), and the chief priests will plot to kill him rather than believe (John 12:10-11). The parable's conclusion is thus both a theological principle and a prophetic anticipation: the problem of unbelief is not evidential but volitional — hardened hearts resist even resurrection.

26. **16:1-31 — The unity of Luke 16:** The chapter divides into two sections — the Parable of the Dishonest Steward (vv. 1-13) and the narrative of the Rich Man and Lazarus (vv. 19-31) — connected by transitional sayings (vv. 14-18). The unifying theme is the use and abuse of wealth in light of eschatological reversal. The steward uses unrighteous mammon shrewdly to secure his future; the rich man uses it selfishly and loses everything. The Pharisees, identified as φιλαργυροι ("lovers-of-money"), stand as the implied audience for both parables. The structural parallel is precise: in the first parable, a rich man has a steward who squanders his possessions; in the second, a rich man is himself the failed steward of God's gifts, who squanders them on daily feasting while Lazarus starves at his gate. Both parables demand an answer to the same question: What will you do with what has been entrusted to you, knowing that an accounting is coming?
